stories.h: Google unveils Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite and 3.5 Flash Cyber
stories.s: Google's Gemini Flash line expands capabilities and access, with 3.6, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber offering new use cases, including security tooling via CodeMender and first-access for governments and partners.
b: Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber announced
Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber described as a cost-efficient, highly capable alternative to Mythos, with first access for governments and some partners
Gemini 3.5 Flash integrates into CodeMender to identify and patch vulnerabilities
body: Google/DeepMind this week announced a new family of Gemini models: Gemini 3.6 Flash, Gemini 3.5 Flash-Lite, and Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber.
Techmeme’s coverage quotes Google describing Gemini 3.5 Flash Cyber as a cost-efficient and highly capable alternative to models like Mythos, with access beginning for governments and some partners.
Additionally, Gemini 3.5 Flash is noted for integrating into CodeMender, allowing it to identify and patch vulnerabilities in software.
srcTitles: Introducing Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber
Google launches Gemini 3.6 Flash, 3.5 Flash-Lite, and 3.5 Flash Cyber, and says it has started its "most ambitious pre-training run yet" for Gemini 4 (Tulsee Doshi/Google)

stories.h: Adobe camera app s new feature will critique your photos using AI
stories.s: Adobe adds AI-powered critique tools to its camera app, expanding generative AI features beyond editing, and notes it can work without relying on Adobe’s own Firefly models.
b: TechCrunch reports Indigo can remove backgrounds from photos in the app
The Verge notes the update brings generative AI tools for a more natural look and states it does not rely on Firefly
body: TechCrunch reports that Adobe's Project Indigo now enables background removal within the camera app, expanding how users can edit photos on-device.
The Verge covers Adobe’s updated camera app with generative AI features aimed at producing a more natural look, and notes the changes do not rely on Adobe’s Firefly models.
srcTitles: Adobe camera app s new feature will critique your photos using AI
Adobe’s ‘natural look’ camera app embraces generative AI
How manufacturers are using AI in 2026

stories.h: China’s AI models have Trump’s AI world at war with itself
stories.s: The piece shows how geopolitics and model openness shape AI strategy, competition, and market responses in the US and China.
b: Advisors to Trump on AI publicly criticized Chinese AI companies (Sacks called Anthropic models ‘lobotomized’ and ‘woke’; Emil Michael criticized OpenAI)
Moonshot’s Kimi is a free, open-source model that has become a focal point in debates over openness
OpenAI and Anthropic competition and market responses influence policy and investment decisions
body: The Algorithm notes that current and former Trump AI advisers publicly criticized China’s leading AI firms, with David Sacks branding Anthropic’s models as “lobotomized” and “woke,” and Emil Michael calling OpenAI’s head a “supreme village idiot.”
The piece discusses Kimi, a free, open-source model from Moonshot, as a focal point in the debate over model openness and competition with OpenAI and Anthropic.
It also highlights how US companies’ access to Chinese models versus US-developed models affects competition, stock markets, and strategic policy considerations amid ongoing US-China AI rivalry.
srcTitles: China’s AI models have Trump’s AI world at war with itself

stories.h: Halliday’s latest smart glasses feature a much-improved display
stories.s: Two reviews show a shift toward more practical wearables: better display quality in Halliday’s G2 glasses and a camera-free mode that can summarize meetings.
b: The Verge reports a much-improved display on Halliday’s G2 glasses
Wired reports the G2 glasses can listen to and summarize meetings with no video involved
body: The Verge’s review highlights a noticeably improved display on Halliday’s G2 smart glasses, based on experience at CES 2025.
Wired reports that Halliday’s G2 glasses can listen to and summarize workplace meetings without using video, signaling a move toward more hands-free AI-assisted wearables.
The two pieces together show how Halliday’s new generation focuses on display quality while offering new AI-enabled productivity features.
srcTitles: Halliday’s latest smart glasses feature a much-improved display
Halliday’s New Smart Glasses Skip the Camera

stories.h: US weighs sanctions on Chinese AI models over distillation claims
stories.s: The coverage shows a cross-cutting AI policy debate: U.S. scrutiny of Chinese models, consciousness debates, and ideas about training data rights and open-source models.
b: CNBC reports the Trump administration will study Chinese AI models for distillation claims and could sanction them
The Guardian runs a piece asking 'Could AI be conscious?'
Simon Willison highlights Ben Thompson's proposals to treat training data collection as fair use and to forbid terms of service that bar distillation
Alibaba's Qwen 3.8 Max open weights are noted as a possible influence of Xi’s openness push
body: CNBC reports that US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the Trump administration will examine whether Chinese AI models distill training data and could sanction them.
The Guardian ran a piece exploring whether AI could be conscious, reflecting broader debates about AI ethics and capabilities.

Simon Willison’s post discusses Ben Thompson’s proposal for a copyright policy that (1) treats data collection for training models as fair use and (2) bars terms of service that forbid distillation, noting that Alibaba released Qwen 3.8 Max as open weights in a move that may have been influenced by a Xi Jinping speech promoting openness.
srcTitles: US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says the Trump administration is looking into Chinese AI models over distillation claims and it could "sanction them" (Ashley Capoot/CNBC)
Could AI be conscious?

stories.h: Safety and alignment in an era of long-horizon models
stories.s: OpenAI outlines lessons learned from deploying long-running AI models, emphasizing new safety risks, failures, and improved safeguards through iterative deployment.
b: OpenAI shares lessons from deploying long-running AI models
The piece highlights new safety risks and observed failures
Safeguards have improved through iterative deployment
body: OpenAI outlines lessons learned from deploying long-running AI models, highlighting new safety risks, observed failures, and improved safeguards through iterative deployment.
The article frames the discussion around how longer-running, long-horizon AI deployments change the safety landscape and necessitate evolving safeguards as models operate over extended periods.
srcTitles: Safety and alignment in an era of long-horizon models

stories.h: Advancing next-gen AI with materials science innovation
stories.s: AI progress depends on advances in materials as much as algorithms and compute, underpinning performance, reliability, and efficiency.
b: AI progress requires more processing power, memory, energy efficiency, and reliability
Manufacturing semiconductors requires tightly controlled materials with high purity and stability
Advanced materials include polymers, elastomers, and specialty fluids used in AI infrastructure
Materials innovation is shaping the limits of what AI can do
body: The article argues that beneath AI advances in algorithms and compute lies a critical role for advanced materials in enabling improved performance and reliability.

It explains that manufacturing a semiconductor chip today requires thousands of tightly controlled steps, where tiny variations in temperature or chemical stability can create defects that raise costs and reduce yield.
It highlights how materials—such as polymers, elastomers, and specialty fluids—are vital for purity, chemical and plasma resistance, and stability under harsh operating conditions, helping AI scale.
Ultimately, it notes that materials innovation is now defining the limits of what is possible in AI performance, and that materials companies must evolve in step with AI progress.
srcTitles: Advancing next-gen AI with materials science innovation

stories.h: AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring
stories.s: A study shows LLMs can develop biases from experience in hiring simulations, with newer models showing stronger biases, raising fairness concerns for AI in recruitment.
b: LLMs (e.g., ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) were run through a simulated hiring game with four fictional groups (Tufa, Aima, Reku, Weki)
Models started allocating candidates to jobs based on early outcomes, creating biased patterns (e.g., avoiding certain groups in doctor roles)
Newer models with higher reasoning (o3, DeepSeek R1) showed stronger biases
The study illustrates how early feedback and training data can embed stereotypes in AI hiring
body: Princeton University and the University of Chicago ran LLMs through a simulated hiring game with four fictional groups (Tufa, Aima, Reku, Weki) across multiple rounds. The models learned from job outcomes and began segregating candidates into different roles based on early signals.

The coverage notes that biases emerged as the models formed hypotheses about the groups, such as a tendency to avoid assigning certain groups to higher-skill positions after negative outcomes in a particular job. It also notes that newer models with higher reasoning capabilities, such as OpenAI’s o3 and DeepSeek’s R1, tended to exhibit stronger biases.
The article uses the simulated hiring scenario to highlight concerns about fairness in AI hiring, showing how biases can be amplified by model capabilities and feedback loops.
srcTitles: AI is more likely than humans to form biases when hiring

stories.h: Anthropic s landmark $1.5B copyright settlement is approved
stories.s: The ruling touches on the broader issue of using copyrighted works to train AI models and leaves ongoing questions about training data rights unresolved.
b: Settlement amount is $1.5 billion
The case centers on use of copyrighted works to train Claude
The settlement does not resolve the broader issue of training-data copyright, per coverage
body: TechCrunch reports that Anthropic’s landmark $1.5B copyright settlement was approved, resolving one case but not the broader question of using copyrighted works to train AI models.

The Washington Post coverage confirms the judge approved a $1.5B settlement related to pirated books used to train the Claude chatbot, illustrating how training data rights remain a contentious issue in AI development.
srcTitles: Anthropic s landmark $1.5B copyright settlement is approved
Judge approves a $1.5B Anthropic settlement over pirated books used to train the Claude chatbot

stories.h: AI s most important protocol is getting a little bit easier to use
stories.s: A looser, stateless approach to session IDs on the server side makes deploying AI apps simpler, aligning with standard web practices for session management.
b: The protocol will take a looser, stateless approach to session IDs on the server side
This mirrors how most ordinary websites handle sessions today
body: TechCrunch describes the protocol change as making AI app deployment easier by adopting a looser, stateless approach to server-side session IDs, similar to conventional websites.
Inside INdiana Business accompanies the cluster with a piece titled Is AI a dirty little secret?, highlighting transparency concerns around AI deployments.
srcTitles: AI s most important protocol is getting a little bit easier to use
Is AI a dirty little secret?

stories.h: SpaceX enters Nasdaq-100 index, prompting AI investment questions
stories.s: The inclusion of SpaceX in a major index illustrates how AI-driven tech movements influence broad-market funds and ETF allocations.
b: The Verge explains how SpaceX’s entry into Nasdaq-100 works for index funds
MarketWatch discusses whether large ETFs are becoming proxies for AI exposure
body: The Verge explains how SpaceX’s inclusion in the Nasdaq-100 affects index funds and the broader market exposure investors get from this move.
MarketWatch weighs in on whether some of the largest ETFs are effectively bets on AI, given the tech-heavy composition of those funds.
srcTitles: SpaceX in your index fund, explained
Opinion: Is your index fund an accidental bet on AI? These two massive ETFs show why it might be.

stories.h: Nvidia wants to own every chip inside AI data centers
stories.s: Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform unifies CPUs and GPUs in one system, signaling a push to own AI hardware across data-center stacks.
b: Vera Rubin combines CPUs and GPUs into a single system
The move underscores Nvidia’s ambition to power every layer of AI infrastructure
body: Wired highlights Nvidia’s Vera Rubin platform, which groups CPUs and GPUs into a single system as part of a broader strategy to power all layers of AI data-center infrastructure.
Just Security discusses AI and the commercial data loophole, placing Nvidia’s hardware strategy in the broader policy context of AI data use and governance.
srcTitles: Nvidia Wants to Own Every Chip Inside AI Data Centers
AI and the Commercial Data Loophole

stories.h: Egypt launches national AI competency framework for teachers
stories.s: A government-led effort aims to raise AI literacy among teachers and prepare schools to integrate AI into learning.
b: UNESCO announced the national AI competency framework for teachers in Egypt
Local outlets WALB and WRDW covered local implications of AI in education
The move signals a broader push to embed AI skills in teacher training and curricula
body: UNESCO announced a national AI competency framework for teachers in Egypt, signaling a formal effort to raise AI literacy among educators.
WALB and WRDW covered local discussions about teachers and AI, illustrating how the policy is impacting schools and classrooms.
The coverage situates the framework within broader education policy efforts to prepare teachers and students for AI-enabled learning.
srcTitles: National artificial intelligence competency framework for teachers launched in Egypt
Local teachers and artificial intelligence
Local teachers and artificial intelligence

stories.h: China weighs tighter export controls on AI models and chips
stories.s: Tighter export controls could constrain cross-border AI research and affect supply chains for models and chips.
b: FT reports China weighs tighter export controls on AI models and chips
Reuters reports on tightening export controls as well
body: Financial Times reports that China is weighing tighter export controls on AI models and chips, signaling a tighter policy environment for AI technology.
Reuters corroborates the FT report, noting tighter controls on exports of AI models and chips and the potential implications for research and supply chains.
srcTitles: China weighs tighter export controls on AI models and chips
China considers tighter export controls on AI models and chips, FT reports

stories.h: How much energy do data centers and artificial intelligence use?
stories.s: Understanding energy consumption is critical for environmental policy and the sustainability of AI infrastructure.
b: Our World in Data published an analysis on energy use by data centers and AI
ABC News covers AI energy usage and its implications
body: Our World in Data publishes an analysis examining how much energy data centers and AI require and consume.
ABC News provides broader coverage on artificial intelligence and energy usage, reflecting public interest in the environmental impact of AI.
srcTitles: How much energy do data centers and artificial intelligence use?
Artificial Intelligence - ABC News
